data.title: Ponce Financial Group's Quality Shines Through (Upgrade)
data.description:
Over the last few months now, one bank that has done exceptionally well from a share price appreciation perspective has been Ponce Financial Group, Inc. (PDLB). The stock is up 16.3% since I reaffirmed the business as a Hold candidate back in the middle of November of last year. By comparison, the S&P 500 (SP500) is up just 5.8%. This move higher came as a result of major improvements in revenue and profitability. The company is seeing a nice expansion in its net interest margin thanks to changing market conditions and continued shifts in the composition of its balance sheet.

After seeing the improvements that the company exhibited, I must say that I should have upgraded it sooner. The firm is well positioned for a declining interest rate environment despite the fact that most of the loans on its books are variable in nature. Management has already demonstrated meaningful improvements when it comes to both the balance sheet and the income statement. And with asset quality as high as it is, I think that upgrading it to a soft Buy is appropriate here.

Of course, that picture could change as new data comes in. And it just so happens that, on April 24th, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of its 2026 fiscal year. We unfortunately don't have any estimates when it comes to analysts. But given how the company has performed recently, I would wager that 2026 will start to look healthier for the company than 2025 did.

Some Stellar Results

As of the time of this writing, the newest data that investors have regarding Ponce Financial Group covers through the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, deposits came in at $2.05 billion. This represents a nice jump over the $1.90 billion that the company reported a year earlier. This is in spite of the fact that brokered deposit exposure for the institution remains quite low. Only about 3.3% of all deposits fell under the brokered category at the end of 2025. This is also positive because it means that the company's expansion has been organic in nature. And it is also great because brokered deposits tend to be more costly than organic deposits are.

One other thing I would like to point out about the deposit situation of the institution is the fact that only $454.5 million, or 22%, of all of its deposits are uninsured. I typically like to see this number come in no higher than 30%. So on that front, Ponce Financial Group is doing quite well.

As deposits increase, the value of loans can increase also. Today, loans amount to about $2.60 billion. About a year earlier, that was $2.29 billion. The most significant exposure that the institution has here would be the $854.1 million tied up in construction and land loans. But not far behind would be the $756.5 million invested in multifamily residential properties. There are some other meaningful categories also, such as non-residential properties at $526.2 million and investor-owned assets at $307.3 million. But on the whole, the loan situation for the institution seems stable.

It would be unwise for us to ignore other parts of the balance sheet also. Take, if you will, securities. From 2023 to 2024, these dropped from $581.7 million to $472.9 million. And by the final quarter of 2025, they had dropped even more to $365.2 million. At the same time as this, cash and cash equivalents fell from $139.8 million to $126.2 million. And debt remained flat at $596.1 million. What this all indicates to me is that management has made a deliberate decision to go almost all in on loans. The goal here is certainly to improve overall profitability. And honestly, that does seem to be working out nicely.

In the chart above, you can see the net interest margin for the company for the most recent quarter compared to the same time last year, as well as for 2025 compared to 2024. There was a nice improvement year over year. This came as the yield on loans for the institution increased and as a cutting of the interest rates by the Federal Reserve allowed the firm to pay not only debtholders less, but depositors less also. The fact that securities are yielding less is evidence to me that management made a wise decision to shift some of their assets from securities to loans.

In the chart above, you can see the end result of the growth of the company's balance sheet and the changes in its composition combined with improvements in the interest rate environment. Net interest income increased, working to push net profits up nicely. The $9.9 million in net income for the final quarter of 2025 translates to an annualized rate of profitability of $39.6 million. In the chart below, you can see what this means for the business on a price-to-earnings basis. If instead of using the results from 2025, we use projected figures for 2026, you would get a price-to-earnings multiple of 10.7. That is, unfortunately, still a bit above the 10 maximum that I typically like to see. However, only three of the five banks that I compared it to in that chart are cheaper than it is. And one of those is just barely cheaper. So compared to other similar firms, I would say it is comparable in this regard.

In the chart below, you can see a very similar type of analysis using the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples. On a price-to-book basis, the institution is actually near the more expensive end of the spectrum. Four of the five institutions I stacked it up against are cheaper than it is. But on a price-to-tangible book basis, only two of them ended up being cheaper. When you average all of this out, you do end up with a firm that is, once again, around the middle of the group.

This is a common theme when it comes to Ponce Financial Group. In the first chart below, you can see a return on assets reading for the business of 1.26%. That is actually above the 1% minimum that I typically like to see. But with two of the five businesses that I compared it to having a reading higher than what it does, it is once again near the middle of the group. The subsequent chart below that shows the same type of analysis using return on equity. Once again, two of the firms are superior to it, even though the 12.50% reading that we get is comfortably above the 10% minimum that I like to see.

Probably my only issue with the business would be its credit quality metrics. Unfortunately, 1.02% of its loans and 0.83% of its assets were classified as non-performing at the end of the most recent quarter. These numbers are not only above the 0.87% and 0.69%, respectively, that the business saw a year earlier. They also happen to be well above the 0.50% maximum that I like to see. So this certainly needs to be monitored moving forward.

As I mentioned at the start of this article, management will soon be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. That should occur on April 24th. Unfortunately, analysts have not provided any estimates for the business. But for context, in the first quarter of 2025, revenue was $24.6 million. Earnings per share amounted to $0.25, which translated to net profits of $5.7 million. Considering how the company has performed as of late, I wouldn't be surprised if the numbers this year are higher.

The last thing I would like to touch on is that, for those looking for a declining interest rate environment, Ponce Financial Group is certainly worth considering. In the image below, you can see what would happen to net interest income over the course of a year if the business were to be hit by a 1% drop in interest rates. In this case, we would see a bit of an increase. In fact, up until a 300 basis point decline in interest rates, you would actually see net interest income rise. This is intriguing when you consider that, for all loans that come due more than one year out from the most recent quarter, only 36.6% have fixed rates. But because of the changes that the business would see in the cost of deposits and debt, the decline associated with adjustable-rate loans is more than offset.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, it is my sincere belief that I should have upgraded Ponce Financial Group previously. I'm not just saying that because of the missed upside. Rather, it has to do with the fact that management has done a solid job as of late. Moving forward, I expect that the company will benefit from declines in the interest rate environment, even if that is short term. And when you look at asset quality and other factors such as growth, I think that upgrading it to a soft Buy is warranted.

data.title: Madison Air Solutions: Good Growth, But Overvalued Stock
data.description:
Madison Air Solutions Corporation (MAIR) listed its stock on the NYSE on the 16th of April. The IPO has turned out to be successful; following the listing, Madison Air stock trades at $33.7 as of writing, well above the company’s $27 price for the stock offering. Fast growth in data center cooling solutions is driving optimism for the investment, and seemingly for good reason.

The indoor air quality company has managed to grow well during 2025, boosted by AI data center investments alongside other revenue streams' growth. Madison Air’s financial performance will likely remain good, but ultimately, the stock already prices in very ambitious expectations. I believe that investors are likely better off waiting for a better entry point.

An Introduction to Madison Air & Its Financials

Headquartered in Chicago, Madison Air manufactures and sells indoor air quality products. The company’s brands include Nortek Air Solutions, Big Ass Fans, Reznor, AprilAire, and a number of other names, through which Madison Air sells products such as heating, air filtration and purification, humidification, air conditioning solutions, and other products. Around two thirds of Madison Air’s revenues are from commercial customers, and the remainder is from residential customers.

Madison Air estimates that the company holds around an 8% market share of a large $40 billion TAM in North America; Madison Air’s market position is noteworthy alongside AAON, Inc. (AAON), Vertiv Holdings Co (VRT), Trane Technologies plc (TT), and other key competitors. The company has acquired a total of 13 businesses since 2017 to grow its market potential. M&A activity is a core factor in Madison Air’s strategy.

Madison Air’s recent financial performance has been very attractive. The company generated $3.3 billion in revenues in 2025, growing by 27.3% year-on-year from 2024. Underlying organic revenue growth has been approximately 7.8% from 2020 to 2025. Good revenue growth has been coupled with high margins; Madison Air reported a very good 16.8% operating margin in 2025. The company requires minimal CapEx to grow, making Madison Air’s free cash flow strong.

Madison Air does have quite a high amount of leverage from debt; the company’s total interest-bearing debts stand at $5.6 billion after 2025. Proceeds from the company’s IPO offering should help lower leverage, though, in my opinion making the company’s balance sheet sustainable.

The Focus Point Is on Madison Air's Data Center Growth

Madison Air should have good earnings growth ahead as well. The company manufactures cooling solutions for data centers through Nortek Data Center Cooling, creating a clear platform for growth – AI data center investments are projected to nearly triple from $600 billion in 2026 to $1.7 trillion by 2030 already according to Dell’Oro. Competitor AAON has clearly captured growth from the data center market as I’ve written in previous articles, and Madison Air is positioned to grow similarly. Madison Air enters 2026 with over $2 billion in the backlog, up by 108.6% year-on-year.

Ultimately, it’s still quite unclear how significant the data center cooling market is as a share of Madison Air’s current revenues, and how far the revenue stream can grow. Madison Air hasn’t laid out any specific financial targets yet as the IPO is very recent; extrapolating significant growth is a dangerous game. I believe that data center growth, and growth in other offerings, does create quite a good platform to grow on.

MAIR Stock Is Too Expensive; I Suggest Caution

I built a discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Madison Air to estimate a fair valuation for the stock. Due to quite limited investor relation communications so far, the model’s estimates are quite challenging to make.

As a base scenario, I expect revenue momentum to remain good organically as data center revenues grow. I estimate a 2025-2035 CAGR of 9.6% with 3% perpetual growth after 2035.

I expect margins to rise slightly over time through general operating leverage; I estimate the EBIT margin to ultimately stabilize at 17.5%, up by 70 basis points from 16.8% in 2025. Madison Air’s minimal CapEx and non-cash amortization should make the company’s cash flow conversion from EBIT very good going forward as well.

The estimates put Madison’s fair value at $24.2, suggesting -28% downside as of writing. I believe that the stock’s post-IPO valuation is too expensive; despite ambitious cash flow growth expectations, Madison Air doesn’t seem to be worth its current price. Investors are likely better off waiting for a cheaper entry point. The company was willing to offer shares at $27 in the IPO offering, making $33.7 a not very appealing entry level.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 8.79%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 6.22% interest rate using Madison’s 2025 debt and interest expenses. I assume a fair 20% long-term debt-to-equity ratio; Madison Air’s IPO offering should help moderate leverage.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.26% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I use the engineering & construction industry’s beta estimate of 1.21 for Madison Air. With a liquidity premium of 0.3%, the cost of equity stands at 9.62% and the WACC at 8.79%.

Takeaway

Madison Air seems like an exciting company following the company’s IPO offering. The company has grown well as data center cooling solutions are taking off, and other revenue streams perform well as well. Ultimately, with a limited history and no publicly available financial targets, Madison Air’s future growth rate is uncertain, but the stock already prices in very ambitious growth. Due to an unattractive valuation, I initiate MAIR stock at a Sell rating.

data.title: Red Cat: A Multi-Domain ISR Drone Winner In The Making
data.description:
During the war in Iran, we saw that neighboring countries were attacked with Shahed drones and ballistic missiles. It once again re-emphasized the need for missile defense and cost-efficient counter-drone solutions. Much to my surprise, it was drone stock companies that had limited to no counter-drone capabilities that saw their share prices surge. One of those names with lower scalable counter-drone capability is Red Cat (RCAT). In this report, I take a deeper look at the company’s investment case.

Red Cat Envisions Multi-Domain Unmanned Capability

Red Cat operates with three subsidiaries, namely Teal Drones, Flightwave, and Blue Ops. The company has the Black Widow Short Range Reconnaissance drone, for which it doubled production capacity in 2025. It is a lightweight drone equipped with Electro Optic and Infrared sensors for short-range reconnaissance missions, which would be useful for monitoring, for instance, energy infrastructure and military bases. The drone features on-board AI processing capabilities. The company also has the Edge 130 Blue, which has a 2-hour endurance when operated in fixed-wing mode, providing longer-range surveillance capability.

Blue Ops is where the company focused on multi-domain unmanned platforms. Their Variant 7 is an unmanned surface vessel expanding unmanned capabilities to the sea domain, but ideally Blue Ops is where the company also develops land and submersible unmanned and autonomous solutions. Furthermore,

For now, Red Cat’s main appeal seems to be in drone-powered ISR (Intelligence, Surveillance, and Reconnaissance), but it also has a broader appeal as the company intends its multi-domain products to integrate into a single ISR platform, allowing for data acquired from multiple domains to be visualized together to get a more complete intelligence picture. The company has some counter-drone capabilities in the form of the ACS Bull Frog and the Zeus. The ACS Bull is an autonomous weapon station that can be mounted on the Variant 7. The Zeus is guided ammunition that can also be mounted on the Variant 7. This is basically counter-drone capability from other manufacturers that Red Cat can mount on its own vessel. Where Red Cat attempts to differentiate itself is by providing a unified controller that can be used for all of its products and across multiple domains to control the drones.

Red Cat Is In The Scaling Phase With Gross Margins Flipping Sign

If we look at the company’s financial performance, we see that revenues rose substantially in 2025. We do note that 2024 was a transition period of eight months as the company changed its fiscal year to end in December rather than April. So, that drives some of the increase in revenues, but in 2025, the revenues also increased as the company scaled drone deliveries under the Short Range Reconnaissance program. Gross profit increased by $3.3 million to $1.3 million, indicating that margins swung from -27.4% to 3.2%. SG&A increased due to higher stock-based compensation and labor costs supporting growing production. R&D grew 81% to $17.9 million, and other expenses were up, driven by fair value adjustments on the convertible notes and a non-recurring gain on sale in 2024, bringing the net operating loss to $66.6 million, indicating a 72.5% increase in losses.

What we see clearly is that Red Cat is in the scaling phase with gross margins turning positive, and from there, operating leverage should drive the margins further. A watch item will be the development of operating expenses.

Red Cat Gets A Speculative "Buy" Rating

Typically, I value stocks according to an EV/EBITDA method. However, since Red Cat is not generating positive EBITDA, I am using an EV/Sales method instead. That already makes Red Cat higher risk. At a peer group multiple, we see no upside. However, when applying a trading multiple that is twice that of the peer group, we see around 21% upside. So, I have a speculative "Buy" rating with a $15.24 price target, indicating around 21% upside. What should be noted is that the company will have elevated free cash flow burns this year and next year, and even in 2028, the free cash flow is not expected to turn positive. By that time, the cash position of $167.9 million will also have significantly declined. So, this is a speculative buy but with dilution risk.

Conclusion: Red Cat Is An Autonomous ISR "Buy"

When I saw shares of Red Cat increasing following the drone strikes in Iran, Red Cat was among the companies that showed a positive share price increase. However, the company does not really have a counter-drone solution that is purpose-built. It can leverage anti-drone systems built by other manufacturers, and its first-person view drone could be repurposed for anti-drone operations, but since it is not autonomous, I do not believe it is the most scalable solution. I believe that companies such as EOS have better solutions. However, I do believe that eventually this could be a strong ISR multi-domain provider, and therefore I am marking the stock a speculative "Buy."

data.title: U.S. Crude Remains Under Pressure As Bulls Stay Cautious
data.description:
By Anton Kharitonov

​The oil market is primarily trading as a geopolitical asset rather than a classic supply-demand balance instrument. Any news surrounding the U.S. and Iran, as well as the Strait of Hormuz, triggers sharp upside moves on supply disruption risks and equally rapid pullbacks on de-escalation.

The main source of volatility is the risk of a supply shock in the Middle East. The Strait of Hormuz remains a critical chokepoint for global energy, so even localized deterioration is immediately priced in via a risk premium. This makes the market nervous and thin, with speculative positioning amplifying swings.

Over a longer horizon, price pressure could intensify from rising supply. The U.S. continues ramping up production, with EIA pointing to record levels in 2026, limiting the sustainability of any price rally. A broader market tilt toward oversupply would further cap upside if the geopolitical premium fades quickly.

Global demand remains resilient, with the summer season traditionally supporting fuel consumption. However, at elevated price levels, the risk of demand destruction grows, particularly if the geopolitical premium persists longer than anticipated. Thus, current price support appears more reactive than sustainably bullish.

Amid expectations of U.S.-Iran talks, WTI (CL1:COM) quotes continue facing selling pressure, capping upside attempts at resistance around $87.60-88.40 per barrel. Support at $85.40-85.20 is holding for now, but risks of a breakdown toward $83.00-82.00 remain.

Nevertheless, Middle East escalation risks stay elevated, so bulls should remain extremely cautious.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Coherent: A Cautious Buy Despite Hefty Valuation
data.description:
Stocks of companies with exposure to optoelectronic components designed for AI infrastructure are on fire, and Coherent Corp. (COHR) is no exception. The share price has increased by 88% year-to-date despite quite cold sentiment around the largest technology companies and unfavorable geopolitical narrative. This rally looks totally justified because of Coherent's strong earnings growth momentum backed by the company's well-placed positioning along a lasting AI megatrend. Due to the secular nature of the AI shift and Coherent's improving positioning in the AI era, a significant multiple compression over the next few years is very much on the cards. I believe that COHR is an interesting investment opportunity, and I invite readers to make a deep dive into its fundamentals and valuation together with me.

Fundamental analysis

In Coherent's business overview outlined in the 10-Q report, the company is described as a vertically integrated manufacturing company that develops a broad range of products for use in communications, industrial, instrumentation, and electronics applications. The company's main growth star is its 'Datacenter & Communications' segment, which generated 71.6% of the total revenue during the latest available quarter (FQ2 2026).

This segment's revenue increased by 34% in FQ2 2026 on a year-over-year basis. Such a growth pace looks very impressive considering the company's revenue CAGR of nearly 17% over the past five years. It implies that COHR's current revenue growth pace is twice as high as the historical average. On top of that, the segment demonstrates outstanding results in terms of operating leverage. This extract from the 10-Q report shows that the segment's profit growth is significantly higher than revenue growth. The management cites continued strong AI data center demand as the main growth driver behind this segment's revenue and profit growth.

For this reason, to understand COHR's future business expansion potential, it is important to understand the secular nature of the AI-fueled growth. The lion's portion of this trend depends on investments in AI infrastructure made or planned by hyperscalers. During the calendar Q4 2025 earnings season, all the largest cloud providers (Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOGL)) announced plans to significantly increase investments in data centers. These plans are backed by the fact that AI capabilities already help these giants to open up new opportunities to boost revenue growth. Cloud-related segments of all three of these giants demonstrated accelerating year-over-year revenue growth over the last four quarters, which all of them explain by AI-driven acceleration. Since the introduction of AI capabilities helps giants to drive their financial performance, it is reasonable to expect that the aggressive AI buildout is a structural phenomenon.

Bringing it together, we have a strong, lasting AI-related industry shift and COHR's proven ability to ride this structural wave based on the great performance of its Datacenter & Communications segment. To finalize the fundamental picture, let's assess the company's readiness to further cement its position in the AI era.

COHR looks solid from the financial perspective. Reliance on debt is insignificant mainly because the business is consistently profitable with a solid EBITDA of nearly 20%. Stable profitability and a healthy financial position give the management more options in capital allocation. The company doesn't pay dividends, which is another positive factor in terms of capacity to reinvest in growth and R&D.

Beyond just financials, recent developments related to new products and partnerships also solidify the bullish narrative. In early March, Coherent and Nvidia (NVDA) announced a strategic partnership to develop optics technology to scale next-generation data center architecture. It is a multi-year agreement including a $2 billion investment from Nvidia, which will help Coherent to scale R&D in the domain of optical interconnects. This is a rapidly growing niche, which has the potential to nearly triple in size over the next decade. Coherent's already existing footprint in AI connectivity together with Nvidia's support looks like an endeavor with really high potential to build a new toll collector in AI interconnect. And the cadence of new product releases is really impressive. If we refer to the company's official newsroom, we see that Coherent announces new product breakthroughs nearly every month. Putting it all together, Coherent's potential to create new growth verticals within already strong AI-related narrative is certainly visible.

Mitigating factors

Since Coherent's positioning along a lasting AI megatrend is proven, I prefer to select multiple contraction trajectory as a primary source of getting insights about valuation. Given Coherent's fundamental quality and the recent Nvidia partnership, the probability of achieving strong EPS growth appears to be high.

On this basis, the expected decline of forward P/E from nearly 100 to 64 by the end of FY2026 and to 46 by the end of FY2027 looks realistic. Nonetheless, the long-term FY2028 forward P/E ratio estimate of 34.6 is still significantly higher than the company's historical average forward P/E of 24. Such a relatively high estimated forward multiple for FY2028 implies that the market prices aggressive EPS growth after FY2028 as well. This is a really risky bet because forecasting over more than three years means inherent mounting uncertainty for every next year of this long-term horizon. For example, the modern world is rapidly innovating, and Coherent's connectivity products might become obsolete by the end of the 2020s. There are also various other risks with longer-term horizons, like uncertainties around the macroenvironment, geopolitical developments, and monetary policy. On that basis, Coherent's high forward P/E multiple is a warning sign.

On top of that, Coherent's reliance only on the Datacenter & Communications segment as the primary growth engine creates vulnerabilities and concentration risks. The company has another segment, Industrial. This business is demonstrating quite weak revenue dynamics at the moment due to various factors. Apart from natural cyclicality, it is crucial to mention that the management has been selling some parts of this segment recently, like the Aerospace & Defense and Materials Processing Tools divisions. Such de-diversification to focus on strengthening the AI-related business might be reasonable amid the early innings of the AI megatrend but might create long-term vulnerabilities when the AI-related growth starts slowing down.

Conclusion

Despite its hefty valuation, I consider COHR a cautious Buy. The main reason is that Coherent's impressive fundamentals increase the probability of new positive earnings revisions. In this case, there will be a meaningful multiple contraction wave. On top of that, the calendar Q1 2026 earnings season might bring new announcements from hyperscalers that they are once again upgrading their AI CapEx plans for 2026-2027.

data.title: United Community Banks: Q1 Earnings And New Acquisition Are Not Quite Enough
data.description:
United Community Banks, Inc. (UCB) released its Q1 2026 earnings report before the market opened today. The bank holding company announced adjusted earnings of $0.70 per share, in line with expectations on net income of $84.3 million. Diluted GAAP earnings of $0.69 per share was an improvement of $0.11, or 19.0%, from Q1 2025, but a decrease of $0.01 from the final quarter of last year.

Earnings were only part of today’s story for United Community Banks, however. The company also announced an agreement to acquire Peach State Bancshares in a stock and cash transaction that is expected to close in Q3 of this year. The total value of the deal is estimated to be $100.8 million, not a huge commitment considering UCB’s market cap currently stands at $4.1 billion. Peach State Bancshares operates only two branches of Peach State Bank and Trust, both in North Georgia.

This announcement is consistent with United Community Banks, recent history of expansion via acquisitions. Its most recent deal previously took place last May when it added the assets and operations from ANB Holdings, the parent of American National Bank. The deal was also a relatively small one with an initial value of approximately $80 million, giving UCB greater penetration in markets in South Florida.

United Community Banks is averaging almost one acquisition per year over the last decade. The company is now ranked as the 72nd largest bank in the U.S. by the Federal Reserve. Bigger does not always mean better, however, and there just is not enough in UCB's most recent earnings for consider the bank as a standout from its regional banking peers. I consider UCB stock to be a Hold for the reasons discussed below.

Company Overview

Headquartered in Greenville, South Carolina, United Community Banks is the holding company for United Community Bank which has branches in six Southern U.S. states – Tennessee, North Carolina, South Carolina, Alabama, Georgia, and Florida. The company offers traditional banking services to both individual and commercial clients as well as wealth management advising and fiduciary services to high-net-worth customers. One of the biggest positive aspects of the company is its geographical territory in the Southeast which includes some of the fastest growing state economies in the U.S. United Community Bank has branches located in the nine fastest growing major metro areas in the Southeast as well as nine of the top 15 mid-sized metro areas in the Southeast. (There is another regional bank in Illinois called United Community Bank that is employee-owned, with over 100 branch locations. It has no connection to UCB.)

As of March 31, United Community Banks controlled $28.2 billion in assets. That total included a securities portfolio valued at $5.8 billion and gross loans of $19.6 billion. UCB’s loan portfolio grew 1.1% in the first three months of 2026 and 6.4% in the last 12 months. A breakdown of the loans held by the bank by category can be seen below.

Non-owner occupied commercial real estate, or CRE, is the largest line item at $5.0 billion, or 25.4% of the loan mix. Owner occupied CRE is next at $4.0 billion, or 20.6%. UCB has $3.1 billion (15.9%) outstanding in residential mortgages and another $2.8 billion (14.1%) committed to commercial and industrial loans. No other loan type has a share of the portfolio greater than 10%.

The bank also held deposits worth $24.0 billion at the end of Q1 2026. This gives UCB a loan to deposit ratio of 81.7%, about 3.8% higher than the average for a bank of its size. Non-interest-bearing accounts account for 26.9% of the bank’s deposits, which helps the company maintain a cost of deposits that is well below the national mean. While the FDIC data for Q1 2026 is not yet available, the cost of deposits for all banks in Q4 2025 was 1.99%. For UCB, it was just 1.76% during the same period.

The stock for United Community Banks currently has a yield of 2.93%, six basis points ahead of the median for the financial sector. Last year was the eleventh in a row that the company has raised its annual payout. The current quarterly distribution is $0.25 per share and increases are usually announced for the third quarter of each year.

Analysis of United Community Banks

In addition to the modest earnings improvements noted at the start of this analysis, there were other positive data points to consider from UCB’s Q1 2026 report. Net interest margin for the quarter was 3.65%, three basis points better than Q4 2025 and 29 better than Q1 2025. The company’s adjusted quarterly return on assets remained flat from Q4 2025 at 1.22%, but that was 18 basis points better than Q1 from last year. The bank’s adjusted efficiency ratio of 55.7% showed a 57 basis point improvement from the same period last year.

After a rough patch at the end of 2025, asset quality for UCB appeared to be on more solid footing at the end of Q1. Net charge-offs fell 12 basis points to 0.22% in the last three months. Past due loans dropped nine basis points to 0.14%. The combination of special mention and substandard loans ended the quarter at 2.9%, the lowest mark since Q2 2024. Nonperforming assets did tick up slightly from 0.48% in Q4 of last year to 0.50% in the most recent quarter.

There are other signs of strength from United Community Banks worth noting from previous quarters. The company made strong efforts to clear up its balance sheet last year with two separate actions. On September 15, UCB redeemed all outstanding shares of its Series I preferred stock. This transaction was conducted with cash on hand for a total cost of $88.3 million. UCB redeemed two series of senior debentures that carried a total value of $135 million, eliminating all of its outstanding senior debt. This allowed the company to enter 2026 with a simpler capital structure.

From a valuation standpoint, United Community Banks falls short of what I would call a bargain. The recent financial results from the bank seem to fall largely in the category of average, but its valuation seems to be slightly above that range. The company earned $2.71 per share on an adjusted basis in FY 2025. This year, UCB is expected to record a profit of $2.98, 10% higher than last year. The forward PE of 11.5 is slightly higher than the sector median, yet the growth expected from the company does not seem to support the higher valuation, even if the difference is a small one.

The tangible book value for the stock was $22.56 at the end of the last quarter. That is only 1.4% more than at the end of Q4 and just under 10% higher than at the end of Q1 2025. At its current share price of $33.33, UCB is trading at a price to TBV ratio of 1.48, ahead of the most recent average for all banks in the U.S., which was approximately 1.37 at the end of March.

Risks to Consider

The bank is exposed to added risk through its loan portfolio that is comprised of a large share of commercial real estate. When non-owner occupied and owner occupied CRE are added together, they make up 46.0% of the total loan mix for UCB. These loans are highly sensitive to any downturns in economic activity in the areas where they are issued. Decreases in consumer or commercial spending could increase default rates and ultimately take away future profit opportunities for the company.

United Community Banks also has an equipment financing subsidiary, Navitas, that is responsible for about $1.9 billion, or 9.7%, of the company’s outstanding gross loans. These loans come with a much higher yield than the others in UCB’s portfolio, but the higher yield comes with greater risk of default. The ratio for net charge-offs for these loans is 1.25%, over 12 times the rate for the other loans for the company.

The final risk to consider before making an investment in any regional bank would be an unexpected change in the interest rate environment that could impact the company’s ability to increase net interest income. This year started with the expectation that the Federal Reserve would cut short-term rates at least twice this year, but the odds of that happening seem to be getting longer as inflation creeps back up close to 3%. Higher long-term rates could impact UCB’s ability to write new loans, especially home mortgages if the 30-year rate continues to stay above 6%. Weaker consumers could lead to smaller deposits, greater lapses in loan payments, and a general contraction of activity for the bank.

The Bottom Line

The merger with Peach State is expected to add roughly $0.09 to UCB's earnings per share in FY 2027, the first full year of its inclusion in the company's operations. United Community Banks may repurchase shares in the open market to offset the dilution from the merger, and that could add another $0.03 to FY 2027 profits. While this acquisition certainly adds to the company's market penetration in Georgia, it does not change my opinion of the company overall. Waiting for such a relatively small improvement that is over a year away is not enough to move the needle for me to give the UCB a Buy rating at the current time.

My Hold rating is not intended to be a statement of real pessimism for the company as much as it is an expectation that its stock will likely trade in line with its regional banking peers. The risks and the rewards seem to be relatively balanced here.

data.title: Beyond Meat: Highly Risky Business With Near-Zero Gross Margins
data.description:
The stock market is still hovering near all-time highs despite uncertainty over a resolution for the Iran conflict. Amid the sudden surge in optimism for U.S. equities again, small and mid-cap stocks have surged, with many reversing a large chunk of this year's losses.

But stock selection in this space is key, as many low-quality businesses have also benefited from a rebound. I put Beyond Meat (BYND) in this category. The plant-based meats manufacturer has rallied nearly 40% this year, with shares skyrocketing after the company's recent Q4 earnings print.

I last wrote a sell article on Beyond Meat in December, when the stock was trading at ~$1.30 per share. Initial enthusiasm that investors had toward the company's debt restructuring (which involved retiring nearly $1 billion in convertible notes and exchanging it for equity, giving Beyond Meat a longer lifeline but also substantially diluting existing investors who had shares prior to the refinancing) has waned, as Q4 gives us even more evidence that there is little demand for Beyond Meat's products. I'm reiterating my sell rating here.

The major issue I see with Beyond Meat is now that the company has bought itself 1+ years of liquidity and a lifeline, attention is shifting to how (if at all) Beyond Meat will be able to recover its core business, and what catalysts may rescue this stock out of its doldrums.

What we see Beyond Meat doing more and more frequently is testing new form factors for its plant-based meat. For example, recently in mid-April the company launched its new lineup of Beyond Meat Breakfast Sausages, which has a blue-chip base of committed retail partners to sell the product, including Kroger (KR) and Sprouts Farmers Market (SFM), with the company noting it would add Amazon's Whole Foods (AMZN) shortly after launch. Earlier this year as well, the company launched its first-ever protein drink, Beyond Immense, which is meant to capitalize on the huge diet trend toward high-protein meals.

While we appreciate the fact that Beyond Meat is trying everything it can to revitalize its brand, there is little evidence that product newness will solve the company's issues. We believe its declining financials support the thesis that plant-based meat was a mere dietary fad that is fading, especially amid inflation pressures today. Continue to avoid this stock and invest elsewhere.

Beyond Meat Q4 results

Let's now run through Beyond Meat's latest quarterly results, as well as our assessment of how much lifeline Beyond Meat has left, in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Beyond Meat's revenue declined -20% y/y to $61.6 million, missing Wall Street's expectations of $62.0 billion (-19% y/y) by a one-point margin. Even more concerning is the fact that Beyond Meat's revenue decelerated seven points versus -13% y/y in Q3. This serves to highlight the issue that the major financing Beyond Meat pulled off (and which investors cheered) in November gave the company additional time to get its act together, but it did little to change the fundamental trajectory of the business and its declining popularity in consumers' eyes.

Overall, the company sold -22% pounds of product in volume terms, which was partially offset by a 3.5% increase in price. We note here, however, that the company attributed "price" increases to FX advantages from a weaker dollar as well as a higher-priced product sales mix. Actual trade discounts, which is the margin that Beyond Meat passes to its retail partners, increased, reflecting Beyond Meat needing to cede favorable terms to its grocery partners to persuade them to carry its inventory.

The chart below showcases the company's revenue by channel. While the U.S. held up relatively stronger at -11% y/y, international sales fell -32% y/y.

On a relative basis, U.S. retail is Beyond Meat's strongest channel, at a -6.5% y/y decline (while everything else declined at greater than 20% y/y). We note that this does not yet include any impact from the company's new drinks lineup, for which we'll see results in Q1.

The company is leaning on the new drink product to transition its brand to becoming a "plant protein" company rather than just plant-based meats. It notes that this new drink is suitable for GLP-1 users. Per CEO Ethan Brown's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Our first foray in this broader delivery of the power of plants to consumers is our exciting new drink platform Beyond Immerse. The Beyond Immerse platform, a clear and slightly carbonated beverage, is designed to provide the consumer with protein, fiber, antioxidants and electrolytes, effectively immersing the body in the nutritional benefits of plants. We launched Beyond Immerse as we now plan to do with all new retail innovation on the Beyond Test Kitchen to early fanfare and excitement, generating over 3 billion media impressions and selling out of our first limited-run inventory quickly. Beyond Immerse is formulated to support muscle health and recovery, gut health, immune function and hydration. Each serving contains 10 or 20 grams of protein, 7 grams of fiber, and only 60 or 100 calories depending on the level of protein.

Beyond Immerse is made without added sugar, sugar alcohols, artificial sweeteners or flavors, stabilizers, carrageenan and many other ingredients present in many popular protein drinks. Easier to drink than a thick protein shake and made without whey so it's dairy free, the product is designed for the casual to competitive athlete as well as the busy student or professional who wants protein, fiber, antioxidant and electrolytes at the gym, home, work or on the go. Moreover, we believe it is particularly well suited for GLP-1 users."

Still, we note the company is entering into a very crowded category, dominated by incumbents like Optimum Nutrition. We think the company has a weakened brand image that is unlikely to translate into strong drink sales.

On the profitability front, gross margins skidded to just 2.3%, barely above breakeven and falling 11 points relative to 13.1% in the year-ago Q4. Meanwhile, adjusted EBITDA losses in Q4 widened 2.7x y/y to -$69.9 million, reflecting a -114% margin against revenue:

In FY25, Beyond Meat's operating cash burn was -$144.9 million, and after tacking on -$12.3 million spent on capex, total free cash burn was -$157.2 million (which is similar in scale to full-year adjusted EBITDA losses of -$179.3 million).

Beyond Meat's latest balance sheet, meanwhile, carried $203.9 million in cash, counterbalanced against $420.7 million in debt.

For the near term, at current burn rates, Beyond Meat is secure. It likely won't have to raise any additional capital in FY26, but unless it cures its burn rate, it will nearly run out of cash in FY27, when its first tranche of debt is due.

BYND stock - Upside risks and key takeaways

Beyond Meat is betting on the protein drink category (another potential fad, in our view) to drive a resurgence in its brand, and the stakes are high: despite the company's recent refinancing extending its lifeline, sales continue to deteriorate while the company burns through ample cash, pinned down by a near-zero gross margin.

Of course, there are potential upside catalysts that we need to be aware of as well. There's a chance that with the right marketing splash, Beyond Immense will help to drive revitalization for Beyond Meat's sales, and potentially be the "gateway" product that encourages fans to try plant-based meats as well. The drink category is also likely to carry higher gross margins than plant-based meats, which may help the company's profitability issues on top of ongoing investments into manufacturing efficiency.

But in my eyes, this is not a sustainable company, and risks skew to the downside rather than to the upside. I have little faith in the stock's ability to sustain its YTD gains. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: UnitedHealth Is Back In A Big Way
data.description:
UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) has had a tough year and a half, with huge increases to cost ratios, management shifts, and concerns over reimbursement. However, we have been on watch for confirmation of an earnings inflection the last few quarters since Q2 2025. We now have that confirmation. UNH is back. We did learn recently that there would be a better than expected increase in Medicare Advantage service reimbursement rates coming. That started a rally in shares, and then we had the market rally broadly with the Iran ceasefire. Today shares are higher thanks to the just-reported Q1 earnings and the outlook. Let us discuss.

UnitedHealth Q1 Earnings Headline Results

The quarter was a double-line beat here, folks. This is an exciting result. And it was not a mild beat either; it was pretty significant. Sure, compared to a few years ago, the power is not very impressive, but we are in a new world now for this stock and similar names in the pace. We all knew it would be very earnings-light compared to years past. But it was a little better than last year, catching many by surprise. For total revenue, we thought it would be flat. But UnitedHealth Group's Q1 revenues grew 2% year-over-year, to $111.7 billion. This revenue print was a surprising beat of $2.06 billion over what was expected.

UnitedHealth Q1 Earnings Segment Revenues

We thought for Q1 there would be $84-$85 billion in revenue for UnitedHealth Care overall and $60-$63 billion for Optum revenues overall (that is, all three Optum lines of Optum Health, Insight, and RX combined). Of course, for total reported revenue, you always back out so-called eliminations. We expected declines in members in response to cost trends and controls. That is, we are looking for the company to try and be more efficient in the next few years, even if it means fewer members.

UnitedHealthcare's Q1 revenues grew $1.7 billion from last year to $86.3 billion, with about 49.1 million people now served domestically. Optum's Q1 revenues of $63.7 billion were down slightly from $63.9 billion last year. Overall revenues were led by Optum Rx once again. Optum Health saw Q1 revenues of $24.1 billion, which were down 3% from last year, but that was because of fewer members. Optum Insight saw $5.1 billion in revenues versus $5.0 billion last year. And of course, Optum RX was the bulk of revenue here with $35.7 billion, which was up 2% from last year. Despite fewer members, there was growth in specialty pharmacy.

Overall, the Q1 earnings from operations were $9.0 billion. This is a massive sequential improvement from the $0.4 billion in Q4 and on par with the $9.1 billion last year. While we did expect sequential improvement, we were viewing it more in the likely $7 billion range. This led to much higher EPS than expected. On a GAAP basis, EPS was $6.90, and as adjusted, it was a strong $7.23, crushing estimates by $0.63. We also saw progress on the medical cost ratio.

The Medical Care Cost Ratio

The critical metric we were waiting for was the medical care cost ratio. A year ago it was a high 84.8%, and then for the rest of 2025 it had pushed toward 90%. In Q3 it was 89.9%, while in Q2 it was 89.4%. In the sequential Q4, it was 89.1%. But major progress was made here in Q1. That cost ratio came down to 83.9%. Strong improvement. Now, that included a 20 basis point positive impact from Optum Health loss contracts reserve, but still. This is solid progress. We believed that 2026 would see an inflection lower on the ratio, and we are getting those results now.

Outlook Improves

Folks, the initial guide coming into the year was an EPS outlook for $17.75+ in 2026. In our last column, we told you that we were “looking for $18-$19 and possibly getting to $20 in a real bull scenario.” Well, our expectations are still in play. The company now sees adjusted EPS of greater than $18.25. So the new floor is in line with what we expected initially and is likely to be higher if we can see ongoing cost control. We think revenues will be at least $440 billion, with an operating margin that creeps toward 6%. This can get us to the $20 in EPS level. Further, we expect at least $18.0 billion in cash flow. While no guidance was given for the cost ratio for the year, we think the mid-80s is likely. We are off to a great start.

Finally, we would like to point out the company expects to repurchase at least $2.0 billion of its stock by the end of Q2, which adds some bullish pressure. We remain positive on UNH stock.

data.title: Natgas Under Pressure From Oversupply And Globalization
data.description:
By Anton Kharitonov

The U.S. natural gas market is in an inter-seasonal lull, where fundamental imbalances between supply and demand dictate price action. Short-term movements remain weather-driven, but deeper structural shifts are tied to LNG export globalization and geopolitics.

Europe has begun actively injecting gas into storage facilities to prepare for next winter. According to Gas Infrastructure Europe data, EU underground storage fill rates stood at around 28% as of early April 2026, with net injections exceeding 2 billion cubic meters since April. This bolsters demand for U.S. LNG, providing a price cushion into the summer.

Risks in the Strait of Hormuz remain a key source of uncertainty, while the U.S. continues ramping up production amid a policy push for fossil fuels. EIA forecasts market production to hit a record 120.8 billion cubic feet per day (Bcf/d) in 2026, peaking in 2027. Inventories exceed the 5-year average, intensifying oversupply and short-term price pressure.

U.S. LNG exports are reaching historic highs, reshaping market dynamics. New terminals are expanding capacity, making U.S. gas prices increasingly tied to European and Asian demand. Additional support comes from rising power consumption driven by AI and data centers, where gas serves as the primary balancing fuel.

Weather remains the dominant volatility driver. Against this backdrop, natgas quotes recovered losses from prior sessions and tested resistance near $2.73, only to face renewed selling pressure and retreat to support at $2.65. In the near term, prices may trade within this range, with the downtrend intact. A break above resistance would target $2.80.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Rocket Lab: A Buy Ahead Of SpaceX IPO
data.description:
Investment Thesis

As capital floods into the space sector ahead of a potential SpaceX IPO, attention has shifted sharply, almost aggressively toward the next big thing. Since my last coverage Rocket Lab's (RKLB) stock performance was muted and now RKLB is caught in the crosscurrents of rising IPO speculation and capital rotating across the space sector. However, behind the scenes, fundamentals continue to improve as the company remains busy building up its order book and margins while positioning itself in the defense and space infrastructure businesses.

Vertical Integration + Backlog Expansion is a Moat

Rocket Lab is expected to deliver a topline of $185 million-$200 million in Q1 with GAAP margins between 34%-36% and non-GAAP margins between 39%-41%. Rocket Lab can face expenses from operations of $120 million-$126 million and non-GAAP expenses from operations fall between $106 million-$112 million.

Along with income from interest of $8 million, Rocket Lab may hit EBITDA losses of $21 million-$27 million with the share count at 605 million. This stock total has 46 million Series A Convertible Participating Preferred Shares. I am factoring the conversion of 23 million shares from notes into the share count. Compensation from stock ranges from $16 million-$18 million, amid Rocket Lab schedules the peak of research spending for Neutron during the period.

In my view, Rocket Lab may have a shift in spending from research into inventory throughout FY26. Thus, cash consumption may continue to persist due to Rocket Lab Neutron development investments. Beyond that, there can be a boost in Electron launches and HASTE missions. Here, topline from the Space Systems segment mixes with topline from the Launch Services segment. Even with the retirement of the Trinity equipment line that eliminates indebtedness, cash flow will remain at a loss.

Moreover, I see that Rocket Lab works on an integrated manufacturing and transportation business model. It is internalizing the production of space components that yields margin expansion and topline predictability. At FY25’s end, Rocket Lab hit a $1.85 billion backlog, in that, the Space Systems segment has 74%. The $816 million Space Development Agency Tranche 3 prime contract award led to a portion of this backlog boost.

Additionally, Rocket Lab has done acquisitions to internalize component supply chains. Rocket Lab’s integrations (Geost, Optical Support and Precision Components) have internalized electro-optical sensors, optical instruments and precision machining processes. I think owning these production layers reduces vendor bottlenecks. Removing third-party supplier margins reduces production costs and lower production costs expand profit margins. For FY25, Rocket Lab derived a 34.4% GAAP gross margin that represents a 7.8%-point expansion YoY.

Further, the Space Systems segment derived $402.8 million in FY25 topline. Here, capturing prime contractor status for the Space Development Agency shifts Rocket Lab towards an end-to-end mission provider (from a component supplier). How? In-house production of spacecraft buses and optical payloads accelerates timelines for assembly. So, as Rocket Lab is controlling the manufacturing schedule, it may support production matching to government delivery mandates.

As government space architecture programs need fast iteration and constellation deployments, I believe Rocket Lab’s internalized supply chain may match this volume requirement with profitability as scaling production through internal facilities absorbs fixed overhead costs. For this, Rocket Lab allocates manufacturing overhead across a volume of Rocket Lab Electron launch vehicles and spacecraft units. Thus, the increased frequency of launches has enhanced economies of scale, bringing down the cost per launch from $5.7 million in FY24 to $4.8 million in FY25.

Also, margin expansion is coming from this internalized production as Rocket Lab avoids paying premiums to external vendors for optical and mechanical parts. I see that producing spacecraft components internally keeps capital within Rocket Lab and, with that, the $1.85 billion backlog supports multi-year topline visibility. As per Rocket Lab’s management, it may convert 37% of this backlog into topline during FY26. This conversion rate leads to a baseline for future cash inflows as Rocket Lab secures multi-launch agreements and bulk spacecraft orders by providing a consolidated procurement channel.

In my opinion, constellation operators prefer single-source contracting to reduce integration risks. Here, Rocket Lab manufactures the launch vehicle, the satellite bus and the payloads. So, this end-to-end framework may continue to capture contract value and here Rocket Lab is retaining the profit margins at every layer of the spacecraft lifecycle.

Finally, Rocket Lab may have FY26 Space Systems growth as ongoing component manufacturing capacity expansion supports this growth. Rocket Lab’s move on scaling the precision machining complex in New Zealand expands volume limits. With that, Rocket Lab's bringing optical-assembly and testing into its own facilities limits external lead times.

Therefore, as Rocket Lab is internalizing these aerospace supply elements, this protects its financials from macro issues. How? Rocket Lab controls its own production pace and this operational control leads to smooth deliveries. As a result, these Deliveries fulfill backlog obligations that derive topline flow and expand gross margins. All this backs RKLB stock’s valuation as Rocket Lab’s vertical integration isolates its operations from supply chain disruptions as owning the manufacturing processes confirms parts availability.

Finally, the Space Systems segment will scale without waiting on 3rd-party components and this will be reducing external dependencies to keep margins within Rocket Lab. This stable margin expansion over time boosts the profitability on backlog orders.

Neutron Delays Extend Cash Burn Risk

On risks, I see Rocket Lab faces large cash consumption under expanding operating losses from the Rocket Lab Neutron development program. For FY25, Rocket Lab had a net loss of $198.2 million with operating cash flow ended at -$165.5 million.

Also, Q4-FY25 alone led $64.5 million of this cash outflow. I believe that Rocket Lab's financial growth depends on funding the Rocket Lab Neutron medium-lift vehicle. Why? Research expenses boosted to $270.7 million in FY25 that is 55% YoY growth. The Rocket Lab Neutron Stage 1 tank ruptured during a January FY26 hydrostatic pressure trial as a manufacturing defect in the hand-laid carbon composite structure led to the failure.

So, this anomaly forced Rocket Lab to delay the targeted Neutron maiden launch to Q4-FY26. Here, delaying the launch extends Rocket Lab’s timeframe for research spending and it has to allocate more CapEx-to-revenue to manufacture a replacement tank using an automated fiber placement machine. Now as per its management, Rocket Lab may reach peak research spending during Q1-FY26.

Moreover, extended development schedules increase cash burn rates as Rocket Lab has -$114.2 million in non-GAAP FCF during Q4-FY25. CapEx hit $156.3 million for FY25 and Rocket Lab uses this capital to construct Launch Complex 3 in Virginia, engine test facilities in Mississippi and production infra in Maryland. Stabilizing this buildout demands liquidity and here Rocket Lab funds these and operating deficits through equity issues.

During FY25, Rocket Lab derived $1.12 billion in net proceeds (as per 10-K) from ATM equity offerings and issuing over 30 million shares dilutes existing stock. Rocket Lab’s outstanding share count now boosted to 589.5 million shares by FY25’s end. I believe that funding operations through equity pushes Rocket Lab towards capital market volatility. If macro conditions tighten, securing any additional funding for Rocket Lab may become cost-prohibitive.

Overall, even after Neutron reaches the launch pad, Rocket Lab’s financial pressures may persist, in my opinion. As per Rocket Lab’s management, there will be low/negative gross margins for early Rocket Lab Neutron flights. Thus, absorbing fixed overhead costs needs a high launch cadence but Neutron will take years to reach target margin profiles. Until Rocket Lab Neutron hits a stable launch trend, the vehicle will be a margin drag on the financials.

Now, the delay to Q4-FY26 compresses the timeline to capture medium-lift market share. In my view, constellation operators demand exact deployment schedules and missing the FY26 timeline may hurt future contract awards for Rocket Lab. With that, competitors like SpaceX, United Launch Alliance and Blue Origin that are building medium-lift vehicles may capture these constellation contracts.

Takeaway

As capital is chasing SpaceX IPO hype, Rocket Lab continues to quietly compound with growth in its backlog, better margins and increasing vertical integration. While the near-term cash burn will continue, the long-term potential for the company is in realizing full-stack economics.
